subject: math
title: PSLE Math — Percentage Traps (reverse, compound, mixed)

stats.headline: Percentage problems are worth 4-5 marks each on PSLE Paper 2 — and they hide the same traps every year. Once you adopt the unitary method (100% → 1% → answer) and learn the "100% of what?" question, you can crack any compound or reverse percentage problem in one line.

keyConcepts.title: Welcome — Percentage Traps
keyConcepts.body: Percentages look simple — until two of them stack. "20% discount then 10% GST" is NOT 10% off. "+20% then −20%" doesn't bring you back to the start. The fix is one habit (the unitary method) and one question (100% of what?).
bullets:
**The 5 patterns :**
-- 🔵 **Pattern A** — sale price (reverse %) -- 🟢 **Pattern B** — increase then decrease isn't zero -- 🟠 **Pattern C** — successive discounts on different items -- 🟣 **Pattern D** — % and fractions -- 🔴 **Pattern E** — WHAT? (different base)

**The unitary % method :**
Step 1: Identify what is "100%" (the original total).
Step 2: Convert what you KNOW into its % equivalent.
Step 3: Divide to find what 1% equals.
Step 4: Multiply by 100 to find what 100% equals.
💡
the ONE question "100% of what?"

**The 5 patterns PSLE rotates:**
-- 🔵 **Pattern A** — find the original from the sale price (reverse %).
-- 🟢 **Pattern B** — percentage increase then decrease isn't zero.
-- 🟠 **Pattern C** — two successive discounts on different items.
-- 🟣 **Pattern D** — mixed % and fractions (nested remainders).
-- 🔴 **Pattern E** — % of WHAT? (different base).

**The unitary % method PSLE marking schemes always use:**
Step 1: Identify what is "100%" (the original total).
Step 2: Convert what you KNOW into its % equivalent.
Step 3: Divide to find what 1% equals.
Step 4: Multiply by 100 to find what 100% equals.
💡 Example: "85% → $57.80, so 1% → $0.68, so 100% → $68."
Next slide: the ONE question to ask every time you see a percentage — "100% of what?"

keyConcepts.title: The 1 question — '100% of WHAT?'
keyConcepts.body: Every percentage refers to a 100%. The trap is when there's MORE THAN ONE possible 100% in the problem. Pause and write down what the 100% is, in words, before doing any arithmetic.
bullets:
**Examples :**
-- "20% discount on a $50 shirt" → 100% = original shirt price = $50.
-- "Sold 25% of his stickers" → 100% = his original stickers.
-- "Earns 15% more than Ben" → 100% = Ben's earnings -- "Sale price after 20% off is $80" → 100% = original price ( ).

**The mini-rules :**
-- "X% MORE than Y" → Y is 100%, you're at (100 + X)%.
-- "X% LESS than Y" → Y is 100%, you're at (100 − X)%.
-- "X% OF Y" → Y is 100%, you're at X%.

**Examples of finding the 100%:**
-- "20% discount on a $50 shirt" → 100% = original shirt price = $50.
-- "Sold 25% of his stickers" → 100% = his original stickers.
-- "Earns 15% more than Ben" → 100% = Ben's earnings (not his own!).
-- "Sale price after 20% off is $80" → 100% = original price (UNKNOWN — solve for this).

**The 3 mini-rules around 100%:**
-- "X% MORE than Y" → Y is 100%, you're at (100 + X)%.
-- "X% LESS than Y" → Y is 100%, you're at (100 − X)%.
-- "X% OF Y" → Y is 100%, you're at X%.
⚠️ **The killer trap:** after one step, the base changes. "Increased the price by 20%, then discounted by 20%" — the 2nd "20%" is of the NEW price, not the original. The 100% shifted.
💡 **Strategy:** at every step in a multi-step problem, write down what's currently 100%. If it changes, mark it clearly. This single habit prevents Patterns B, C, D, E from going wrong.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern A — Reverse % (find the original)
keyConcepts.body: The question gives you the AFTER price and asks for the BEFORE. The trap: students multiply by (1 + discount) instead of dividing by (1 − discount). The unitary method makes the right answer automatic.
bullets:
**📝 Worked example :**
After a **15% discount**, a jacket cost **$57.80**

**📝 Worked example from PSLE 2024:**
After a **15% discount**, a jacket cost **$57.80**.
Find the original price.

**❌ The trap answer:** $57.80 × 1.15 = $66.47. Wrong.
(Adding 15% back to the sale price gives a different base — not the original.)
💡 **The "÷ by what's left" shortcut:** if 85% → $57.80, then 100% → $57.80 ÷ 0.85 = $68. Same answer in one step.

**📝 Worked example from PSLE 2018, museum:**
A museum had **9000 visitors** in August. This was a **20% increase**
from July.
Find July's visitors.
After +20%, August = **120%** of July. 120% → 9000 → 1% → 75 → 100% → **7500**.
(Naïve: 9000 × 0.8 = 7200 — wrong, because 9000 is already 120% of the original.)

keyConcepts.title: Pattern B — Increase then decrease isn't zero
keyConcepts.body: "Price went up 20%, then down 20%." Most students say "back to the start." Wrong — you end up LOWER. The second percentage is taken of a LARGER number.
bullets:
**📝 Worked example:**
A shirt was originally **$100**.
Price went **up 20%**, then later **down 20%**
💡 ** ** BIGGER number

**📝 Real PSLE 2019 (Lily's savings):**
Jan = $50. Feb = +20% → **$60**. Mar = −15% of Feb → 60 − 9 = **$51**.
Mar vs Jan = +$1 = **2% increase** (not 5%).

**📝 Worked example:**
A shirt was originally **$100**.
Price went **up 20%**, then later **down 20%**.
Find the final price.

**Step 1 — After +20%:**
+20% of $100 = $20.
New price = **$120** (this is the new 100% now).

**Step 2 — After −20%:**
−20% of $120 = $24 (NOT $20 — the base shifted).
Final price = 120 − 24 = **$96**.
💡 **Why $96 not $100?** The 20% decrease was of a BIGGER number ($120 not $100), so the decrease "amount" is LARGER than the original increase ($24 not $20). Result: 4% lower overall.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern C — Successive discounts (% on different items)
keyConcepts.body: Two items, different discount %s, same original price. The trap: students average the discounts. The fix: think in "percentage of original you paid" — then sum those.
bullets:
**📝 Worked example :**
Xinyi bought 2 identical fans (same original price).
She got the 1st at **55% off** and the 2nd at **70% off**.
Total paid = **$127.50**
**❌ The trap :** Average discount = (55 + 70) ÷ 2 = 62.5%, so paid 37.5% of $2 × original

**✓ "Percentage paid" approach:**
Fan 1 at 55% off → paid 45% of original.
Fan 2 at 70% off → paid 30% of original.
Total paid as % of one fan: 45% + 30% = **75% of one fan's original price**

**📝 Worked example from PSLE 2021, Xinyi's fans:**
Xinyi bought 2 identical fans (same original price).
She got the 1st at **55% off** and the 2nd at **70% off**.
Total paid = **$127.50**.
Find the original price of one fan.
**❌ The trap answer:** Average discount = (55 + 70) ÷ 2 = 62.5%, so paid 37.5% of $2 × original. Messy and wrong because the discounts don't average correctly.

**✓ "Percentage paid" approach:**
Fan 1 at 55% off → paid 45% of original.
Fan 2 at 70% off → paid 30% of original.
Total paid as % of one fan: 45% + 30% = **75% of one fan's original price**.

**Solve:**
75% of one fan = $127.50 → 1% = $1.70 → 100% = **$170** (original price of one fan).
💡 **Why this works:** you're paying 45% for one and 30% for the other, both of the SAME original price. So adding 45 + 30 = 75% of one original gives the total cost in % terms.

interactiveQuiz.label: Pattern A — Reverse %
interactiveQuiz.stem:
After a 25% discount, a bag costs $90. What was the original
price?
interactiveQuiz.explanation: After 25% off → paid 75% of original. 75% → $90. 1% → $1.20. 100% → $120. (Distractor $112.50 = $90 × 1.25 — the classic mistake of adding 25% back to the sale price, which uses a different base.)

interactiveQuiz.label: Pattern B — Increase then decrease
interactiveQuiz.stem:
A shirt's price was increased by 25% and then decreased by
20%. The final price compared to the original price is:
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Let original = $100. After +25% → $125. After −20% of $125 → $125 − $25 = $100. Final price = original = unchanged! (This is a special case — 1.25 × 0.80 = 1.00. Most increase-then-decrease pairs are NOT a wash, but +25% then −20% happens to cancel exactly.)

keyConcepts.title: Pattern D — Mixed % and fractions
keyConcepts.body: Some problems mix % and fractions in the same chain — "spent 60% then 1/2 of the remainder". Treat them as nested fractions (Pattern from Nested Fractions class). The "left after each stage" shortcut still works.
bullets:
**📝 Worked example :**
Peggy spent **75%** of her money on a calculator.
Then **40%** of the remainder on a book.
She had **$6.90 left**

** **
After calculator: 100 − 75 = **25% left** After book: 100 − 40 = **60% of that left**
⚠️ ** ** of the original

**📝 Worked example from PSLE 2023, Peggy:**
Peggy spent **75%** of her money on a calculator.
Then **40%** of the remainder on a book.
She had **$6.90 left**.
How much money did Peggy have at first?

**Step 1 — Fraction left after each stage.**
After calculator: 100 − 75 = **25% left**.
After book: 100 − 40 = **60% of that left**.

**Step 2 — Multiply (nested fraction shortcut).**
Final left = 25% × 60% = 0.25 × 0.6 = **15% of original**.
Or: 60% × 25% = 15%.

⚠️ **The trap:** students add 75% + 40% = 115% used, which doesn't even make sense (you can't use more than 100%). It happens because they treat the 2nd % as of the original — but the question says "of the remainder."

keyConcepts.title: Top 5 traps to avoid
keyConcepts.body: The most common percentage mistakes at PSLE. Each one typically costs 2-3 marks per paper.
bullets: ⚠️ ** 1 — Reverse % by adding back instead of dividing.**

⚠️ ** 2 — Assuming +X% then −X% cancels out.**
+20% then −20% gives 96% of original — NOT 100%. The second % is of a bigger number.
⚠️ ** 3 — Averaging discounts in successive purchases.**
⚠️ ** 4 — Treating mixed % + fractions as parallel.**
⚠️ ** 5 — Wrong base for "X% of WHAT?"**

⚠️ **Trap 1 — Reverse % by adding back instead of dividing.**
Sale price $80 after 20% off → original is NOT $80 × 1.2 = $96. It's $80 ÷ 0.8 = $100. The 20% you "lost" is of the original, not of the sale price.

⚠️ **Trap 2 — Assuming +X% then −X% cancels out.**
+20% then −20% gives 96% of original — NOT 100%. The second % is of a bigger number.

⚠️ **Trap 3 — Averaging discounts in successive purchases.**
(55% + 70%) ÷ 2 = 62.5% does NOT give "average price paid". Add the percentages PAID (45% + 30% = 75%), not the discounts.

⚠️ **Trap 4 — Treating mixed % + fractions as parallel.**
"60% then 1/2 of remainder" is NESTED, not parallel. Don't add 60% + 50% = 110% (impossible). Multiply what's LEFT: 40% × 1/2 = 20% left.

⚠️ **Trap 5 — Wrong base for "X% of WHAT?"**
"Mei earns 25% more than Ben" → 100% = Ben's salary, not Mei's. "% MORE than" or "% LESS than" → the OTHER person is the 100%.

keyConcepts.title: Try it — Successive discounts & Mixed % + fractions

interactiveQuiz.label: Pattern C — Successive discounts
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Tom bought 2 identical shirts (same original price). He got
the first at 30% off and the second at 50% off. He paid $48
in total. What was the original price of ONE shirt?
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Shirt 1 paid: 70% of original. Shirt 2 paid: 50% of original. Total paid as % of one shirt: 70 + 50 = 120% of original. 120% → $48. 1% → $0.40. 100% → $40. (Distractor $30 = average discount 40% off $50 — wrong method.)

interactiveQuiz.label: Pattern D — Mixed % + fractions
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Mr Lim spent 40% of his money on books, then 1/4 of the
remainder on stationery. He had $36 left. How much money did
Mr Lim have at first?
interactiveQuiz.explanation: After books: 100% − 40% = 60% left. After stationery: kept 3/4 of the 60% remainder = 45% of original. 45% → $36. 1% → $0.80. 100% → $80. (Distractor $60 = added 40% + 25% = 65% used → 35% = $36 → $103 — wrong approach. Distractor $90 = forgot one of the steps.)

keyConcepts.title: Start practising!
keyConcepts.body: Mixed practice drawn from real PSLE-style percentage problems. Trains the reflex of asking "100% of WHAT?" before any arithmetic, and using the unitary method (the marking-scheme format) for every reverse-% question.
bullets: **mix of 10 questions** reverse %, compound %, successive discounts, mixed % + fractions, "% of what?" base mistakes
Wrong answers auto-saved reviewed in 1 week
AI marks unitary % format — partial credit
You'll tackle a **mix of 10 questions** across all five patterns — reverse %, compound %, successive discounts, mixed % + fractions, and "% of what?" base mistakes.
Any Wrong answers are auto-saved and reviewed in 1 week.
The AI marks your working step-by-step in unitary % format — same as PSLE marking schemes — partial credit if you set up the right percentages but slipped on the arithmetic.

keyWords.definition: PSLE's official marking-scheme approach. Step 1: identify what is 100%. Step 2: convert known to its % equivalent. Step 3: divide to find 1%. Step 4: multiply by 100. Example: 85% → $57.80 → 1% → $0.68 → 100% → $68.

Given the AFTER price (sale price), find the BEFORE price (original). DIVIDE by what's left, don't multiply by what was added back. Sale $80 after 20% off → original = $80 ÷ 0.8 = $100, not $80 × 1.2.

The single most important question in any percentage problem. Different sentences refer to different bases — 'X% more than Y' makes Y the 100%, not X. Write down what's currently 100% before every step.
When two percentages stack, the second is taken of a DIFFERENT base (the result of the first). +20% then −20% gives 96% of original, not 100%. The base changes after every percentage step.

For successive discounts on identical items, sum the % PAID, not the % off. Shirts at 30% off and 50% off → paid 70% + 50% = 120% of one shirt's original price. Averaging the discounts gives the wrong answer.

subTopics.label: Pattern A — Reverse % (find original)
subTopics.description: Given the AFTER price, find the original. Use unitary: identify what % of original was paid, then divide. Don't multiply the sale price by (1 + discount) — that uses a different base.

subTopics.label: Pattern B — Increase then decrease
subTopics.description: +X% then −X% does NOT cancel. The second X% is taken of a bigger number, so the absolute decrease > absolute increase. Result is below the original.

subTopics.label: Pattern C — Successive discounts
subTopics.description: Two identical items at different % off. Sum the % PAID (not the % off), then apply to one item's original price. Averaging the discounts is the classic wrong move.

subTopics.label: Pattern D — Mixed % and fractions
subTopics.description: Spent X%, then 1/n of the remainder. Same nested-fraction logic as the Nested Fractions class. Multiply what's LEFT at each stage. Never add % + fraction directly.

subTopics.label: Pattern E — % of WHAT? (base mistakes)
subTopics.description: Different sentences set different 100%-bases. 'X% more than Y' → Y = 100%. 'X% of Z' → Z = 100%. Write down the base BEFORE any arithmetic to avoid using the wrong total.
